content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 11 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 105r–106v | **Source Images:** DEU_011_page_001.jpg, DEU_011_page_002.jpg, DEU_011_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_011_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Love the LORD your God and keep his charge — his statutes, his ordinances, and his commands — always.

**2** Know today — for it is not your children who are speaking with you; they have not seen and do not know — the discipline of the LORD your God: his greatness, his mighty hand, and his outstretched arm,

**3** his signs and the deeds he performed in the midst of Egypt, against Pharaoh king of Egypt and against all his land,

**4** and what he did to Egypt's army, to its horses and chariots — how he swept the waters of the Red Sea over them when they chased after you and the LORD destroyed them, and they have not recovered to this day —

**5** and what he did to you in the wilderness until you arrived at this place,

**6** and what he did to Dathan and Abiram, the sons of Eliab son of Reuben — how the earth opened its mouth and swallowed them up along with their households, their tents, and every living thing that belonged to them, in the midst of all Israel.

**7** Your own eyes have seen all the great works of the LORD that he has done.

**8** Therefore, keep every commandment I am giving you today, so that you will be strong enough to go in and take possession of the land you are crossing over to take,

**9** and so that you will live long in the land the LORD swore to give to your ancestors and their descendants — a land flowing with milk and honey.

**10** The land you are entering to take possession of is not like the land of Egypt, where you came from — where you sowed your seed and watered it with your foot like a vegetable garden.

**11** But the land you are crossing over to possess is a land of mountains and valleys that drinks in water from the rain of the sky —

**12** a land the LORD your God cares for. The eyes of the LORD your God are always on it, from the beginning of the year to the year's end.

**13** If you will indeed listen to my commands that I am giving you today — to love the LORD your God and to serve him with all your heart and all your life —

**14** then I will send rain on your land in its season, both the early rains and the late rains, and you will gather in your grain, your new wine, and your olive oil.

**15** I will provide grass in your fields for your livestock, and you will eat and be satisfied.

**16** Watch yourselves — do not let your hearts be seduced, so that you turn aside and serve other gods and bow down to them.

**17** Then the LORD's anger will burn against you, and he will shut up the sky so that there will be no rain. The ground will not yield its fruit, and you will perish quickly from the good land the LORD is giving you.

**18** Fix these words of mine on your hearts and in your lives. Tie them as a sign on your hands and let them serve as a mark between your eyes.

**19** Teach them to your children, speaking about them when you sit at home and when you walk along the road, when you lie down and when you get up.

**20** Write them on the doorframes of your houses and on your gates,

**21** so that as long as the sky remains over the earth, your days and your children's days may be multiplied in the land the LORD swore to give your ancestors.

**22** If you carefully keep all these commands I am giving you to follow — loving the LORD your God, walking in all his ways, and clinging to him —

**23** then the LORD will drive out all these nations before you and you will dispossess nations greater and more powerful than you.

**24** Every place the sole of your foot treads will be yours. Your territory will extend from the wilderness to Lebanon and from the River Euphrates to the Western Sea.

**25** No one will be able to stand against you. The LORD your God will spread the fear and dread of you over all the land you tread on, just as he has promised you.

**26** Look: today I am setting before you blessing and curse —

**27** blessing, if you obey the commands of the LORD your God that I am giving you today;

**28** and curse, if you do not obey the commands of the LORD your God, and turn aside from the way I command you today and follow other gods you have not known.

**29** When the LORD your God brings you into the land you are going in to take possession of, you are to proclaim the blessing on Mount Gerizim and the curse on Mount Ebal.

**30** Are they not beyond the Jordan, past the road to the west, in the land of the Canaanites who live in the Arabah, opposite Gilgal, near the oak of Moreh?

**31** For you are about to cross the Jordan to go in and take possession of the land the LORD your God is giving you. When you have taken it and settled in it,

**32** be careful to follow all the statutes and ordinances I am setting before you today.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 2 — "Know today — for it is not your children who are speaking with you":** This verse is notoriously complex in Hebrew. The parenthetical sense is: "I'm talking to you who saw it with your own eyes — not to your children who weren't there." Moses distinguishes the eyewitness generation from those who would come after. PNEUMA renders this as a rhetorical interruption — "know today — for it is not your children who are speaking with you" — to preserve the emphasis without losing readers in grammatical ambiguity. The LXX also wrestles with this verse.

**V. 4 — "swept the waters of the Red Sea over them":** The Hebrew yam suf ("sea of reeds," or "Red Sea") is rendered traditionally. PNEUMA uses "Red Sea" as the established English convention. The verb tsippah (covered, overwhelmed) is rendered "swept... over them" for its fluid force. The LXX uses epikalypsen (he covered over/overwhelmed).

**V. 10 — "watered it with your foot like a vegetable garden":** This puzzling phrase refers to Egyptian irrigation by foot-powered water channels (possibly shaduf devices or simply foot-directed flow channels). The contrast is between Egypt's artificial, labor-intensive irrigation and Canaan's rain-fed agriculture. PNEUMA preserves the phrase intact because it is vivid and the imagery is self-explanatory in context.

**V. 12 — "The eyes of the LORD your God are always on it":** Ayin YHWH Eloheicha bah — God's surveillance of the land is expressed as loving, attentive gaze. This is not surveillance as control but as providential care. The contrast with Egypt's land (v.10–11) is sharpened: Egypt needs human engineering; Canaan depends directly on God's attention and rain.

**V. 13–17 — The blessing-curse rain mechanism:** The rain / no-rain framework is the land's covenant indicator. Obedience brings yoreh (early autumn rains, for planting) and malqosh (late spring rains, for harvest); disobedience brings drought. PNEUMA renders "early rains and late rains" directly from these technical terms. This passage became part of the Shema's second paragraph in Jewish liturgy — its daily recitation tied agricultural life to covenantal faithfulness.

**V. 16 — "do not let your hearts be seduced":** The Hebrew yifteh levavkhem — patal means to open, entice, or seduce. The image is of the heart being lured open, made susceptible to false gods. PNEUMA uses "seduced" to capture the erotic-covenantal metaphor that runs through Deuteronomy and the prophets: idolatry as a form of adultery.

**V. 18 — "Fix these words on your hearts and in your lives":** The Hebrew vesam'tem et devarai elleh al levavkhem — "place these words on your heart and in your life (nefesh)." This verse parallels the Shema instruction of 6:6–8 almost exactly. PNEUMA keeps "hearts and lives" (nefesh = life-force/soul) to show the totality of the demand. Verse 18–20 repeat the phylactery and mezuzah instructions from Deuteronomy 6 — Moses doubles down on embodied memory.

**V. 24 — "Every place the sole of your foot treads":** Kol ha-maqom asher tidroch kaf raglekhem bo — a land-promise based on physical movement and occupation. PNEUMA renders this idiomatically: "Every place the sole of your foot treads will be yours." The territorial scope is enormous: wilderness to Lebanon (north), Euphrates (east) to the Mediterranean (west). The LXX follows precisely.

**V. 26–28 — Blessing and curse as moral architecture:** Moses places the two options starkly: blessing (berakhah) and curse (qelalah). PNEUMA keeps "blessing and curse" without softening — the Deuteronomic worldview is a binary choice structure, and the starkness is intentional. The Gerizim / Ebal ceremony (v. 29) will dramatize this visually in chapter 27.

**V. 29 — Gerizim and Ebal:** These twin mountains stand opposite each other near Shechem, in the heart of Canaan. The choice of this location for the covenant ceremony is geographically and symbolically central — Canaan's own landscape becomes the stage for Israel's covenant renewal. The LXX follows the MT location exactly.

— Signed, PNEUMA
